articles.title: SleeperGem Uses Three Malicious RubyGems Packages to Target Developer Machines
articles.author: info@thehackernews.com (The Hacker News)
articles.content:
Cybersecurity researchers have flagged a new software supply chain attack codenamed SleeperGem targeting the Ruby ecosystem after three malicious gems were published to RubyGems with the end goal of serving additional payloads.
The rogue gems are listed below -
- git_credential_manager (versions 2.8.0, 2.8.1, 2.8.2, 2.8.3) - Published on July 18, 2026
- Dendreo (versions 1.1.3, 1.1.4) - Published on October 14, 2017
- fastlane-plugin-run_tests_firebase_testlab (version 0.3.2) - Published on February 06, 2018
"Each malicious release is a loader," StepSecurity said in an analysis. "It fetches a second stage from an attacker controlled Forgejo host, checks whether it is running in a build system and skips if it is, and on a developer machine it drops a native daemon and installs persistence."
One aspect of the attack that immediately stands out is that "git_credential_manager" impersonates the official Microsoft Git Credential Manager, while the other two had been dormant for years before receiving the malicious updates. "Dendreo" was last updated on October 24, 2020, and "fastlane-plugin-run_tests_firebase_testlab" stayed inactive since March 9, 2019, prior to the new versions.
Another defining trait of the activity is that the releases were published directly to the registry without any matching commit or tag in the source projects.
Interestingly, "git_credential_manager" has been added as a dependency to five packages, including "Dendreo" and "fastlane-plugin-run_tests_firebase_testlab," effectively allowing the malicious payload to spread to existing users of the packages -
- Dendreo
- fastlane-plugin-run_tests_firebase_testlab
- slackHtmlToMarkdown
- seo_optimizer
- array_fast_methods
All the aforementioned packages, with the exception of "fastlane-plugin-run_tests_firebase_testlab," are maintained by the same account ("LR-DEV"). The fact that the gem belongs to a different maintainer ("pinkroom") indicates that more than one account was likely compromised to push the rogue versions to RubyGems.
Once installed, the malware embedded in these packages scans the infected system for about 30 environment variables, including those related to GitHub Actions, GitLab, CircleCI, Travis, Jenkins, and Vercel. If any of those are identified, it promptly exits. The check is assessed to be an intentional attempt to avoid running on ephemeral CI runners and ensure it's executed on a developer machine.
In the case of "git_credential_manager," the malicious code is fired when the library is required, causing it to download two payloads from a public Forgejo instance ("git.disroot[.]org/git-ecosystem"): a shell script ("deploy.sh") and a native binary that carries the same name as the tool the gem masquerades as. On Windows, the retrieved payload is executed via PowerShell.
While version 2.8.2 merely stages the payloads, version 2.8.3 of the gem moves to the next phase of the attack. This involves using the install script to launch the binary as a background daemon, after which it establishes persistence using a cron entry and as a systemd user service and queries the sudo and wheel groups.
"If the user can run sudo without a password, the script re-runs itself as root, and when it runs as root it plants a setuid root copy of the system shell at a path chosen to mimic a networking utility," StepSecurity said.
Users who have installed any of the aforementioned gems are advised to treat the machines and associated secrets as compromised. It's also recommended to remove the dropped daemon at "~/.local/share/gcm/," erase the persistence methods, check for a setuid shell at "/usr/local/sbin/ping6," and rotate all credentials.
"A RubyGems account that has gone quiet for six or seven years doesn't look risky to anyone," Aikido Security researcher Charlie Eriksen said. "That's exactly the profile worth taking over. That's where the SleeperGem name comes from: not a planted, long-game attacker asset, but a real, ordinary account that had simply gone dormant, and looked harmless enough to hijack without anyone noticing."
RubyGems as a Data Exfiltration Dead Drop
The disclosure comes more than two months after RubyGems briefly paused account sign-ups after bad actors pushed dozens of malicious packages as part of a coordinated spam-publishing campaign. Around the same time, Socket researchers flagged a parallel campaign that flooded the registry with 150 gems that abused them as a data exfiltration channel.
Earlier this month, Mend.io disclosed details of an undocumented software supply chain attack that employed another set of 14 RubyGems packages to store stolen credential data.
Specifically, a malicious browser extension was found to have harvested credentials through a locally accessible API, packaged the information into valid .gem files entirely within the browser using JavaScript and standard Web APIs, and uploaded those packages directly to RubyGems.org using a hard-coded RubyGems API key.
"The haul included plaintext passwords, SSH private keys, AWS credentials, crypto wallet seed phrases, Social Security numbers, credit card numbers, and bank account details across 63 vault items," Maciej Mensfeld said.
"RubyGems wasn't the delivery mechanism here. It was the dead drop: a trusted, high-traffic domain where stolen data sat until the attacker came back for it, invisible among normal developer uploads."
articles.summary:
Cybersecurity researchers have identified a supply chain attack dubbed SleeperGem, which involves hijacking dormant RubyGems accounts to distribute malicious software payloads that establish persistence on developer machines.

### Key Points
- Attackers compromised multiple RubyGems accounts to push malicious updates to packages including git_credential_manager, Dendreo, and fastlane-plugin-run_tests_firebase_testlab.
- The malware avoids CI/CD build environments, specifically targeting developer workstations to install native daemons and establish persistence.
- Malicious payloads are fetched from a Forgejo host and can escalate privileges to root if the user has passwordless sudo access.
- Five packages were identified as carriers for the malicious dependency, potentially exposing users of slackHtmlToMarkdown, seo_optimizer, and array_fast_methods.
- Security experts recommend that affected users rotate all credentials and manually remove malicious files, including a setuid root shell found at /usr/local/sbin/ping6.

### Why it Matters
This campaign highlights the significant security risks posed by dormant developer accounts, which are increasingly targeted for hijacking to bypass standard security scrutiny. The incident underscores the need for developers to audit dependencies and maintain strict account security to prevent the exploitation of trusted software registries.

articles.title: Exploit brokers pay $500k for WordPress RCEs. I found one with GPT5.6 and $25
articles.author: Adam Kues
articles.summary:
A security researcher used the GPT5.6 Sol Ultra AI model to discover and chain multiple vulnerabilities in WordPress, successfully achieving remote code execution (RCE) in under 10 hours.

### Key Points
- The AI identified a pre-authentication SQL injection by exploiting a validation desynchronization bug within the WordPress Batch API.
- Researchers bypassed API method restrictions by using recursive batch calls, allowing the execution of unauthorized GET requests.
- The exploit chain leveraged in-memory cache poisoning and a cycle-detection gadget to temporarily escalate privileges to an administrator.
- By triggering the `parse_request` hook while holding administrator authority, the AI enabled the creation of a new admin account to upload a backdoor.
- The entire research process, including exploit development, cost approximately $25 in AI subscription usage.

### Why it Matters
This discovery highlights a significant shift in cybersecurity where AI models can autonomously chain complex, disparate vulnerabilities that would typically require days of expert human analysis. As these tools become more capable, organizations must prioritize rapid patching and automated security testing to defend against increasingly sophisticated, AI-driven exploit development.

articles.title: Bitcoin ETFs see new money again, but inflows remain ‘peanuts’ relative to the recent exodus
articles.author: Omkar Godbole
articles.content:
Bitcoin ETFs see new money again, but inflows remain ‘peanuts’ relative to the recent exodus
Bitcoin ETFs have attracted $273 million in new inflows in two weeks, but the total is barely enough to cover a single "slow" week of recent selling.
- U.S.-listed spot bitcoin ETFs have seen $273 million in inflows over the past two weeks, breaking an eight-week streak of outflows that totaled more than $8 billion.
- Analysts say the recent inflows suggest an improvement in ETF flow dynamics.
- The scale is still too small to confirm a strong return of institutional demand.
The U.S.-listed bitcoin 
The spot ETFs pulled in $75.67 million in investor money in the week ended June 17, following a more robust $197.40 million in the preceding trading week, according to data source SoSoValue. That's $273 million in fresh capital in two weeks, following an eight-week streak of outflows that saw investors yank over $8 billion from these funds.
The two-week inflow is a sign of bullish regime change, according to bitcoin and macro insights newsletter Ecoinometrics.
"ETF flows have settled into a much healthier balance between inflows and outflows. Even better, we’re beginning to see longer streaks of inflows reappear," the newsletter's Friday edition said.
"It suggests we aren’t simply looking at a temporary bounce after an extreme bout of selling. The underlying flow regime has genuinely improved," it added.
Similar bullish interpretations are making rounds on crypto social media, cheering the return of the so-called institutional demand.
That interpretation is intuitive given that ETFs, which let investors gain exposure to the cryptocurrency without owning it directly, are widely seen as a cleaner crypto market gateway for institutions. As a result, positive ETF inflows are taken to mean BTC is receiving institutional support, while outflows suggest the opposite.
Bitcoin's price too has stabilized between $64,000 and $65,000 lately, offering hope that a bottom may be in. Prices peaked above $126,000 in October last year.
On the surface, it looks like the tide has turned. However, there is a massive caveat that makes these ETF inflows look like statistical noise rather than a structural shift.
The peanuts reality check
The hype surrounding this $273 million inflow quickly evaporates when compared to the carnage of the preceding eight weeks. During that two-month outflow streak, the market watched billions of dollars walk out the door.
To put the current "recovery" in perspective: the total amount of money that has entered the market over the last 14 days ($273 million) is barely more than the smallest single-week outflow recorded during that eight-week slump, which was $226.84 million in the week ended June 18.
In other words, it took two full weeks of "renewed optimism" just to offset the quietest week of the recent sell-off.
Too early to call a regime change
While analyst optimism about the streak-breaking inflows is understandable, the data remains too weak to support a definitive conclusion that the institutional bid has returned in force.
Until the weekly inflows consistently dwarf the recent outflows, the narrative of a massive institutional rotation back into bitcoin remains more hope than a mathematical reality.
"Watch ETF flows first. A multi-week positive trend would signal the re-entry of institutional capital in a structured manner," crypto analysis firm BRN said in an email.
Econometrics suggested the same, noting that a strong foundation remains contingent on balanced ETF demand in the coming weeks.
For now, the message to investors is clear: the bleeding has stopped, but the patient is still far from running a marathon.

U.S.-listed spot bitcoin ETFs recorded $273 million in inflows over the past two weeks, marking a potential stabilization after an eight-week period of significant capital outflows totaling $8 billion.

### Key Points
- Spot bitcoin ETFs attracted $273 million in new capital over the last two weeks, ending an eight-week streak of net outflows.
- The recent inflows are modest, totaling only slightly more than the $226.84 million recorded during the smallest single-week outflow of the previous slump.
- Bitcoin prices have recently stabilized within the $64,000 to $65,000 range, suggesting a potential market bottom to some analysts.
- Data from SoSoValue indicates that $75.67 million entered the funds in the week ending June 17, following $197.40 million the previous week.
- Analysts from firms like BRN caution that consistent, larger inflows are required to confirm a definitive return of institutional demand.

### Why it Matters
These inflows signal that the recent period of heavy selling has subsided, though the current volume is insufficient to confirm a major institutional recovery. Investors are monitoring these trends closely as a primary indicator of whether the broader cryptocurrency market is entering a sustained period of growth or merely experiencing a temporary bounce.

articles.title: AI Transforms Silicon Valley, Some Tech Workers Face Evaporating Financial Security
articles.author: EditorDavid
articles.content:
The Washington Post describes a mid-tier executive at Meta as one of Silicon Valley's "winners" whose financial security suddenly "evaporated" as their workforce "pushed headlong into AI and heavy job cuts," creating a transformed job market.  "Her ex-husband, a designer at Meta who was laid off in 2020, eventually gave up looking for jobs in his profession. He now lifts boxes at a warehouse."
Layoffs.fyi, which tracks announced job cuts, counts more than 800,000 tech workers laid off since 2022, including large staff reductions in recent months at Meta, Microsoft, Oracle and Amazon... "There's this whole tranche of people who've been quite used to being among the most upwardly mobile in society who are all of a sudden saying, 'Now I'm the guy on the streetâs'" said Oliver Raskin, who founded Silicon Valley market research consultancy Signalcraft Insights and has surveyed attitudes in the tech labor force...   "The rise of AI, especially, is bound to change the workplace radically," [said Georgetown University historian Joseph McCartin]. "But the way it's going to happen is similar to how technology transformed the auto industry."   Ruth Milkman, a labor sociologist at the City University of New York, said that technology workers are getting a dose of what workers in other industries have long complained about: jobs that feel unsteady or rob them of autonomy. "Low-wage workers are used to it," she said...
 
Many layoffs at technology companies are probably a hangover effect from over-hiring in prior years, experts say. And they don't account for a spotty recent increase in hiring in the information industry, which includes employment of software developers and jobs in media and entertainment. Digging deeper, though, some economists say there are signs that Silicon Valley and other technology-reliant parts of the American economy have reached a turning point where they are growing without needing as many people. The notion was encapsulated in a recent talk that ricocheted through group chats across the tech industry: In it, a partner at the start-up incubator Y Combinator heralded a new generation of AI-first companies that will only need human labor for "novel situations," "ethical considerations" and "high-stakes moments."
 
Gad Levanon, chief economist at the labor research nonprofit Burning Glass Institute, said that the number of hours worked in the information sector has dipped since 2022, while the sector's economic output has increased by about 8 percent a year — more than three times the overall growth rate of the U.S. economy. He says the data reveals a sea change in industries, including technology and finance, toward doing more work with the same or fewer people — one that is spreading to other professional classes. "That's the new reality for white-collar and tech-exposed work: output up, headcount flat or down," Levanon said...
 
Raskin, who has worked in the tech world since the late '90s, said that even though the current moment feels unsettling to many, he's hopeful that it's an early chapter in an evolving story. "It's happened many times before," he said, "that something implodes and all these people lose jobs, but then that talent gets cycled into whatever the next thing is — into a new wave of prosperity."
In the article tech entrepreneur Anil Dash quips that Silicon Valley techies are "are guinea pigs for what tech dudes want to do to everyone."

articles.summary:
Major technology companies are shifting toward AI-driven operations, leading to widespread layoffs and a new economic reality where industry output grows while total headcount remains flat or declines.

### Key Points
- Layoffs.fyi reports that over 800,000 tech workers have been laid off since 2022, including significant staff reductions at Meta, Microsoft, Oracle, and Amazon.
- Data from the Burning Glass Institute shows information sector economic output is rising by 8% annually, even as total hours worked have declined since 2022.
- Experts suggest the industry is reaching a turning point where AI-first companies prioritize automation, reserving human labor for high-stakes or ethical decision-making.
- Labor sociologists note that white-collar professionals are increasingly experiencing the job instability and loss of autonomy previously common in low-wage sectors.

### Why it Matters
This trend signals a fundamental shift in the professional labor market where companies prioritize efficiency gains through automation over traditional headcount growth. As this model spreads to other sectors, it may permanently alter career stability and job security for white-collar workers across the broader economy.

articles.title: 4 retro gaming projects that transform dusty old hardware into a nostalgia machine
articles.author: João Carrasqueira
articles.content:
Your old PC or phone can sometimes feel like a burden lying around at home, but there are great ways to put it to good use. Whether it's an impromptu home lab or a simple dashboard for your smart home, there's quite a bit you can do with it. And that includes gaming, even if modern games are too resource-intensive to handle.
Retro gaming is more enticing than ever given the state of the modern gaming industry, and enjoying those old games is extremely easy, even on old devices. So much so that many projects are designed to make this even easier. If you're looking to have fun while making your older devices useful, projects like Batocera and ES-DE may be exactly what you're looking for.
ES-DE, EmuDeck, or RetroDeck
Turn your computer into a nostalgia machine
EmulationStation, or ES-DE, is one of the best projects for turning your old PC into a retro gaming beast, and my first recommendation for anyone looking into this topic. ES-DE is a program that runs on all major desktop operating systems, and it turns your library of old games into a veritable museum of gaming.
ES-DE is just a frontend, but it's highly customizable and capable. The default theme is clean and simple, but it already does a great job presenting the consoles you have access to and the games you have for each one. By using the built-in data scraper, ES-DE also pulls descriptions, box art, screenshots, and even trailers for games in your library, so perusing it becomes a fun nostalgia trip where you can see all these pieces of history before playing them yourself.
EmuDeck and RetroDeck are two projects built on the back of ES-DE, and which you choose depends entirely on how much work you're willing to do. Because ES-DE is only a frontend, you may still need to download the required emulators to actually play the games, and that's what these solutions help with. When you set up EmuDeck or RetroDeck, the necessary emulators are automatically downloaded, plus you get some customization right off the bat with the included custom themes.
RetroDeck is my personal favorite, but it's only available on Linux, so if you want the easiest way to get started, EmuDeck is the way to go. Pure ES-DE requires a little more work on your part, but it's the only option if you're on macOS.
EmuDeck
- OS
- Windows, Linux, Android
Batocera
Why do anything else?
If you're keen on using an old PC just for retro gaming, and you don't really want a full operating system bogging it down, then Batocera may be a solution for you. Batocera is a Linux-based operating system, and it's a very basic one at that, since it's entirely focused on running ES-DE and the necessary emulators to give you that retro gaming fix.
Indeed, this is still a solution based around ES-DE, but this time, you get an extremely lightweight operating system, with a desktop that only exists with the purpose of importing games into your library. You're not going to be browsing the internet or editing documents on any computer running this OS, but that's okay, because it can run especially well on older, weaker hardware. Linux is already lighter than Windows most of the time, but Batocera strips out many components to make it even lighter.
Personally, I find it easier to install ES-DE on a typical operating system, but Batocera is a great option for a living room retro console that isn't going to do anything else, and it's still highly recommended for low-power machines.
batocera
iiSU
A beautiful frontend for Android
Don't have an old PC, but a phone instead? Then iiSU is the project for you. This is a very recent project focused on creating a frontend for your gaming library, much like ES-DE, but with a completely original UI built from the ground up to be intuitive and beautiful.
The UI is clearly inspired by Nintendo's Wii U, which is an odd choice to say out loud, but it actually looks beautiful and has been modernized for higher-resolution screens.
iiSU is an absolutely commendable and very ambitious project. It isn't just a frontend for your games; it also offers many interesting integrations. You can link your Discord account for basic messaging built right into the UI, link to the RetroAchievements websites to see the things you've accomplished in your retro titles, and more. No emulators are included, but you can set it up to use the emulators of your choice for each platform.
Everything in iiSU is presented beautifully, and there's even some soft background music that really adds to the vibe. This, paired with a phone controller, would make for a wonderful portable gaming setup. iiSU is still in early alpha stages, though, so you have to be ready to deal with some quirks for now. Still, the project is incredibly exciting for the future.
iISU
- OS
- Android
RetroAssembly (or RomM)
Stream your retro games to any device
Finally, if you want your old computer to become a server that makes your retro games accessible anywhere, we have RetroAssembly. This is a Docker container you can deploy on any PC or in a home lab, and it organizes your retro game collection and makes it playable on any device on your network.
RetroAssembly is accessible through a web browser, and the data is stored on the server you create, so your games can be accessed on any device as long as the server is running. And importantly, so can your save data, so you can start playing on your PC in your bedroom, then continue right where you left off using your phone on the couch. Not only does the app support typical keyboard input, but on mobile it also offers touchscreen buttons, so you don't need any extras to get started.
This one is best suited if you already have a home lab set up; otherwise, you'll need to keep your old PC running at all times to keep the games accessible. Alternatively, you can start self-hosting more services on it to make it even more useful so that power consumption doesn't go to waste. Either way, this is a great project.
RetroAssembly
Your old devices can become a lot more fun
Whatever route you end up taking, these projects show that an old PC or phone is far from useless, and, in fact, it can become a lot more fun once its main purpose has been fulfilled. Retro games are still some of the best experiences you can have, and they teach you a lot about gaming history, so it's never a bad idea to have them ready to go for some fun.
articles.summary:
Old computers and smartphones can be repurposed into dedicated retro gaming consoles using specialized software projects that organize game libraries and simplify the emulation process for older hardware.

### Key Points
- ES-DE serves as a customizable frontend for managing game libraries, offering metadata like box art and trailers across major desktop operating systems.
- EmuDeck and RetroDeck automate the installation of necessary emulators, with EmuDeck supporting Windows, Linux, and Android, while RetroDeck is Linux-exclusive.
- Batocera is a lightweight, Linux-based operating system designed specifically to turn low-power hardware into a dedicated retro gaming console.
- The iiSU project provides a Wii U-inspired interface for Android devices, featuring Discord integration and support for RetroAchievements.
- RetroAssembly allows users to host a retro game server via Docker, enabling gameplay and save-file synchronization across any device on a local network.

### Why it Matters
Repurposing aging hardware reduces electronic waste while providing a cost-effective way to preserve and enjoy classic gaming titles. These tools lower the technical barrier for entry, allowing users to transform obsolete devices into functional entertainment systems without requiring advanced programming knowledge.

articles.title: Hackaday Links: July 19, 2026
articles.author: Tom Nardi
articles.content:
We’ll start this week off by giving our congratulations to Skyroot Aerospace of India for successfully launching the country’s first privately developed orbital rocket yesterday. The company’s Vikram-1 booster stands 24 m (79 ft) tall and uses a somewhat unusual four-stage arrangement, with the first three stages using solid propellant and the final liquid-fueled stage being responsible for putting the payload into a precise orbit. With this successful launch, India becomes only the third country in the world with a private company capable of performing orbital launches.
On the other end of the spectrum, we have SpaceX’s prototype Starship, which elected not to leave Earth during a last-second (literally) launch termination on Thursday. Aborted launches are, of course, nothing new in the world of rocketry, especially when dealing with an in-development vehicle that has 33 engines that need to fire up at the same moment before it can leave the pad. But this dramatic abort was unique as it was the first time lift-off of the massive 124.4 meter (408 ft) rocket had been called off when the engines were already running.
Onboard systems took advantage of the very narrow window between the time the Raptor engines are switched on and the rocket actually leaves the launchpad to decide that it was not a good day to visit space after all. Word from SpaceX is that two of the Raptor engines on the first stage will be replaced and that they should be ready to make another launch attempt sometime this upcoming week.
While getting rockets off the ground is never easy, one thing that seems to have no trouble going up is the price of gasoline. Even still, Americans seem largely uninterested in electric vehicles, or at the very least, the slate of EVs that are currently available to them — especially now that the $7,500 federal tax credit has ended. Yesterday, TechCrunch ran an article about all the EVs that have exited the US market over the last year due to stagnant sales or import difficulties, and it’s quite a list.
Some of the vehicles, like the Sony-branded Afeela, aren’t exactly surprising. But major players like Honda, Volkswagen, Nissan, and Hyundai also decided not to bring the 2026 models of various EVs in their lineup to the US. Polestar has been forced out of the market entirely due to new import restrictions on Chinese tech. Even Tesla is paring down their offerings by discontinuing their Model X and S vehicles.
Speaking of struggling sales, earlier this week, Amateur Photographer detailed a fairly dire situation over at GoPro. The company, once the undisputed market leader in rugged action cameras, looks like it might fold before the end of the year if they can’t bolster their cash reserves. GoPro’s legendary status for reliability in the most extreme of conditions is still intact, with NASA trusting the company’s cameras to return external views of the Orion capsule during its historic trip around the Moon on Artemis II. But for more mundane pursuits, consumers are increasingly reaching for cheaper alternatives.
One more piece of bad news while we’re at it: OnePlus took to their community forums on Thursday to announce they’ll no longer be selling new phones in Europe and North America. Anyone who owns a OnePlus phone in these territories will still get software support and updates through the originally marketed end date, and the warranty on the hardware itself will still be honored. But after that, they’ll have to find a new company to do business with. While the company wasn’t exactly a household name, they did offer some compelling hardware, and it’s always a shame to see fewer options on the market.
In hopes it will lighten the mood a bit, we’ll leave you with this exhaustive look at the computers featured in 1993’s Jurassic Park, put together by Fabien Sanglard. It covers everything from laptops, which appeared in the background of shots, to the bank of glorious Thinking Machines CM-5 with their iconic arrays of twinkling red LEDs in the park’s control room. There’s even a section that dives into the software side of things, detailing the real-world applications as well as the more fanciful creations. As Fabien notes, the original Jurassic Park novel featured some surprisingly detailed descriptions of the computer tech used at the park, as author Michael Crichton was himself an accomplished programmer.
See something interesting that you think would be a good fit for our weekly Links column? Drop us a line; we’d love to hear about it.
articles.summary:
Skyroot Aerospace successfully launched India’s first privately developed orbital rocket, while SpaceX aborted a Starship test flight and several major technology and automotive companies announced significant market exits.

### Key Points
- Skyroot Aerospace launched the 24-meter Vikram-1, making India the third nation with private orbital launch capabilities.
- SpaceX aborted a Starship launch after the 33 Raptor engines ignited, requiring the replacement of two engines before the next attempt.
- Major automakers including Honda, Volkswagen, and Nissan are pulling various electric vehicle models from the U.S. market following the expiration of federal tax credits.
- Tesla is discontinuing the Model X and S, while Polestar faces a complete exit from the U.S. due to new import restrictions.
- GoPro faces potential insolvency as consumer demand shifts toward cheaper alternatives, despite the brand's continued use by NASA.
- OnePlus announced it will cease selling new smartphones in North America and Europe, though it will maintain existing software support and warranties.

### Why it Matters
The aerospace industry is seeing a shift toward private sector dominance, even as complex engineering challenges continue to cause high-profile launch delays. Simultaneously, the contraction of the electric vehicle and consumer electronics markets suggests a period of significant economic consolidation and changing consumer preferences in Western regions.

articles.title: 3 reasons why this 140W GaN charger took over my desk
articles.author: harish.jonnalagadda@futurenet.com (Harish Jonnalagadda) , Harish Jonnalagadda
articles.content:
I use several chargers throughout the house, and I still rely on the 500W Nexode as my go-to charging station in the office. As good as it is, the charging station is bulky, so I don't use it on my desk. I instead prefer a smaller charger that still gives me the flexibility to charge up to four devices at once, and in recent months, I used Baseus' excellent Nomos 2, which also has a Qi 2.2 mat.
That was before Satechi rolled out its latest GaN charger. The ChargeView 140W is a 4-port charger that's roughly the same size as a regular 140W wall charger, but you get four charging ports in total, and Satechi even managed to fit a tiny panel onto it that shows real-time charging details. The best part is that it costs just $99 on Amazon, which is similar to other 140W chargers I used in recent months. Here are three reasons why the ChargeView 140W is now my go-to desk charger.
The ChargeView 140W is the smallest 4-port charger I used
For me, the number one consideration for a desk charger is the size, and the ChargeView 140W nails the brief. It is smaller than other 4-port charging stations I used, and if anything, it's even smaller than a travel charger.
I also like that it has four USB-C ports; most 4-port chargers include a USB-A port, and while I can understand the reasoning behind that, I don't need the port on a desk charger, so just having a multitude of USB-C ports is highly convenient — at least in my use.
Satechi also includes a stand that lets you orient the ChargeView 140W horizontally or vertically. I used it horizontally, and the stand makes accessing the USB-C ports just a tad more convenient. The charger itself is made out of plastic, and I didn't see any issues with build quality whatsoever.
It has USB 3.2 AVS and goes up to 140W
The ChargeView 140W does a solid job charging four devices at once, and the C1/C2 ports have the ability to charge devices up to 140W. The USB-C1 port uses the latest USB PD 3.2 AVS standard to adjust voltage in minute increments, and it makes a difference while charging the iPhone 17 Pro and iPhone Air.
The USB-C2 port uses the standard USB PD 3.1 profile, and it also goes up to 140W. The C3 and C4 ports go up to a maximum of 40W individually, and these are better-suited for charging handheld consoles and accessories. Like all other GaN chargers, the ChargeView 140W does a good job intelligently managing the power budget based on the number of ports in use. Here's a look at how the power delivering is affected based on number of devices connected:
Get the latest news from Android Central, your trusted companion in the world of Android
I used the ChargeView 140W with my Find X9 Ultra, iPad Pro M4, iPhone 17 Pro, and a dozen accessories over the course of the last month. It did just as reliable a job as other GaN chargers I used, and the smaller size and availability of four USB-C ports makes it a long-term addition to my desk.
You get an LCD panel that shows real-time charging info
It isn't that long ago that brands started adding LCD panels to their chargers to show charging details, and I like this feature a lot. The ChargeView 140W has a tiny panel of its own, and it is a great way to see how fast a connected device is charging.
What I like about this is that you can charge the orientation of the panel based on the charger's configuration. So if you're using it vertically, a long press of the button located at the top switches the orientation. It's a small addition, but it's one that makes a noticeable difference.
On the whole, the ChargeView 140W is a great addition to Satechi's charging portfolio, and if you need a reliable desk charger with the ability to charge four devices at once, I'd suggest getting this one.
The ChargeView 140W is a diminutive 140W GaN charger that effortlessly charges all your tech. It uses the latest USB PD standard, doesn't take up much room at all, and you don't have to pay a premium.
Harish Jonnalagadda is Android Central's Senior Editor overseeing mobile coverage. In his current role, he leads the site's coverage of Chinese phone brands, networking products, and AV gear. He has been testing phones for over a decade, and has extensive experience in mobile hardware and the global semiconductor industry. Contact him on Twitter at @chunkynerd.

articles.summary:
Satechi has launched the ChargeView 140W, a compact four-port GaN charger featuring real-time power monitoring and USB PD 3.2 AVS support for efficient multi-device charging at a $99 price point.

### Key Points
- The ChargeView 140W features four USB-C ports and supports a total power output of 140W.
- It utilizes the USB PD 3.2 AVS standard on the primary port for precise voltage adjustments.
- The device includes an integrated LCD panel that displays real-time charging data and supports orientation switching.
- Satechi includes a versatile stand allowing the charger to be positioned either horizontally or vertically.
- The charger is priced at $99 and is designed to manage power distribution intelligently across multiple connected devices.

### Why it Matters
This charger represents a growing trend of high-wattage, compact GaN accessories that prioritize desk space without sacrificing performance. By offering advanced power standards and real-time monitoring at a competitive price, it provides a practical solution for users managing multiple high-power devices simultaneously.

articles.title: Goldman Sachs flags broken CTA threshold levels in Nasdaq, SPX nearing critical zone
articles.author: Editorial Team
articles.content:
Goldman Sachs flags broken CTA threshold levels in Nasdaq, SPX nearing critical zone
Mechanical selling triggers loom as trend-following funds hit key momentum levels in major equity indices
Goldman Sachs is flashing a yellow light on equity markets. The bank’s trading desk is reporting that short-term momentum thresholds used by Commodity Trading Advisors have been breached in the Nasdaq, while the S&P 500 is sitting uncomfortably close to its own critical level.
What CTA thresholds actually mean
CTAs, sometimes called trend-followers or managed futures funds, don’t have opinions. They have rules. When prices stay above certain moving averages or momentum triggers, these funds are programmed to buy or hold. When prices break below those levels, the funds sell, regardless of fundamentals or macro narrative.
Goldman’s analysis puts the Nasdaq’s short-term CTA threshold at roughly 19,608, a level that has now been breached for the first time since April. The S&P 500’s threshold sits at approximately 5,472, with the index hovering about 3% above a more consequential medium-term level.
The scale of potential selling is the real story
Earlier this year, CTAs were firmly in buying mode. Goldman’s data showed these funds added roughly $23B in US equities in a single week and approximately $53B over a month-long window.
Bank of America put a number on the worst-case scenario: approximately $100B in programmatic equity selling could be triggered if the S&P 500 drops another 3% from current levels. Goldman’s own estimates suggest the global selloff from breached thresholds could reach tens of billions across equity futures markets. The Nasdaq breach is already confirmed. The SPX domino hasn’t fallen yet, but it’s wobbling.
CTA selling doesn’t respond to earnings beats or Fed commentary in real time. It responds to price. If prices fall, the model sells more. If the model sells more, prices fall further.
What this means for markets and for crypto
The absence of crypto from Goldman’s CTA threshold analysis is itself a data point worth noting. Goldman’s coverage is focused entirely on traditional equity indices, with no mention of digital assets or token markets in the context of these threshold reports.
If systematic equity funds are not meaningfully positioned in crypto, their mechanical selling doesn’t directly hit digital asset order books. However, a $100B forced-selling event in S&P futures would test the appetite of even the most conviction-driven crypto buyer. Traders would be wise to watch the SPX’s proximity to that medium-term CTA threshold closely. If 5,472 breaks with conviction, the next few sessions in both equity and crypto markets could get complicated fast.
articles.summary:
Goldman Sachs warns that Commodity Trading Advisors have breached key momentum thresholds in the Nasdaq, potentially triggering significant mechanical selling pressure across major U.S. equity indices like the S&P 500.

### Key Points
- Goldman Sachs reports the Nasdaq has fallen below its short-term momentum threshold of 19,608 for the first time since April.
- The S&P 500 is currently hovering near its own critical threshold of 5,472, which serves as a key trigger for trend-following funds.
- Commodity Trading Advisors (CTAs) utilize automated, rule-based trading strategies that force equity sales when specific moving averages are breached.
- Bank of America estimates that a 3% decline in the S&P 500 could trigger up to $100 billion in programmatic equity selling.
- Unlike fundamental investors, these systematic funds execute trades based strictly on price action rather than macroeconomic data or corporate earnings.

### Why it Matters
These mechanical sell-offs can create downward price momentum that ignores traditional market fundamentals, potentially leading to rapid volatility across global financial indices. While digital assets are not directly included in these CTA models, a large-scale liquidity event in equity markets could negatively impact broader investor sentiment and risk appetite.

articles.title: 13 legal startups to watch in 2026, according to investors
articles.author: Melia Russell
articles.content:
Artificial intelligence has turned one of Silicon Valley's least fashionable sectors into one of its hottest. Investors are pouring money into legal startups, hoping to find the next breakout winner before everyone else does.
For a long time, building software for lawyers felt like a contrarian bet. Investors saw legal tech as too small a category, with slow-moving customers and long sales cycles.
ChatGPT changed the equation. Sales cycles that once stretched for years began collapsing into weeks, as founders raced to apply frontier models to take on work done by attorneys.
Investors from San Francisco to London are now looking to fill a Harvey-sized hole in their portfolios. Funding to legal startups worldwide reached $2.1 billion in the first half of the year, per Crunchbase data.
Business Insider asked a group of venture capitalists to nominate the year's most promising legal startups valued at less than $1 billion. Here are their picks.
Atticus
Nominated by: Ann Crady Weiss, True Ventures (an investor)
Total funding: more than $100 million, according to the company
What it does: Atticus helps disabled and injured Americans figure out what kind of legal help they need, then connects them with the right lawyer or nonprofit to handle their case.
Why it's promising: Atticus is part of a huge but overlooked corner of the legal market. Government and insurance programs distribute more than $1 trillion to individuals each year, but people often struggle to figure out what aid they qualify for or find a lawyer.
Atticus is making that process easier — and scaling quickly. "I've watched them grow from a small fry to the largest provider of legal help to disabled and injured Americans," said Ann Crady Weiss, who sits on the company's board.
Atticus now serves 115,000 people a year and says it has secured more than $7 billion in lifetime aid for clients.
Courtroom
Nominated by: Helen Min, True Ventures (not an investor)
Total funding: Undisclosed
What it does: Courtroom lets litigation teams test their case against simulated juries and judges. Lawyers can upload case materials and see how different arguments, witnesses, and narratives might land.
Why it's promising: Founder Elizabeth Grabowski Parikh learned how litigation teams build a case while working in-house at a startup and pursuing companies accused of infringing its patents. In June, Courtroom launched from stealth with backing from a notable group that included OpenAI employees and Relativity's venture arm.
Helen Min said Courtroom borrows a playbook already proven in marketing: using simulated audiences to test how a message will go over before it goes live. Here, the audience is made up of digital clones — and the stakes are much higher.
Crosby
Nominated by: Abby Meyers, Bain Capital Ventures (an investor), and Aydin Senkut, Felicis (not an investor)
Total funding: $86 million, according to the company
What it does: Crosby is a law firm that sells basic legal services to other companies, mostly early-stage startups. The company pairs lawyers with software it builds on top of frontier models.
Why it's promising: "Rather than selling customers a tool, Crosby delivers a service" from start to finish, investor Abby Meyers said. That could give Crosby an important advantage. Customers may be less likely to replace Crosby with a product from a major lab or try to build a similar tool themselves.
Crosby also learns from every contract it reviews, Meyers said, creating a pool of proprietary data that could help make its service better over time. The company recently worked with Micro1 to build a benchmark for measuring how well models negotiate contracts.
DeepJudge
Nominated by: Aydin Senkut, Felicis (an investor)
Total funding: $53 million, according to the company
What it does: DeepJudge helps lawyers find out what their firms already know. The software searches across documents, emails, and past work, then connects that knowledge to agents and workflows.
Why it's promising: Rather than competing with the likes of Harvey, DeepJudge is positioning itself as the layer that makes Harvey and other tools smarter about how a particular piece of work actually works. Customers include Freshfields, Holland & Knight, Cozen O'Connor, and Greenberg Traurig.
Felicis and Coatue backed the company's $42 million Series A in November. Investor Aydin Senkut said the three founders, all former Google researchers based in Zurich, represent "exactly the kind of technical bar we look for in a category this competitive."
GC AI
Nominated by: Max Abram, Scale Venture Partners (an investor), and Mallun Yen, Operator Collective (not an investor)
Total funding: $73 million, according to the company
What it does: GC AI speeds up contract work for corporate legal teams by helping them draft agreements, review terms, and pull answers from their documents.
Why it's promising: Tools that make lawyers faster can be a tough sell to firms that bill by the hour. In-house legal teams have the opposite problem: Efficiency is part of the job.
GC AI is built for them. The company says it already has close to 1,900 customers.
Founder Cecilia Ziniti brings credibility from having served as general counsel three times. "GC AI feels shaped by someone who has lived the daily grind of the job," Mallun Yen said. That experience shows in a product that's tailored for in-house lawyers, investor Max Abram said, rather than one originally designed for law firms and later adapted for companies.
Laurel
Nominated by: Miloni Madan Presler, IVP (an investor)
Total funding: $153 million, according to the company
What it does: Laurel automatically tracks how lawyers spend their workdays and turns that activity into timesheets, sparing them from piecing together their billable hours at the end of the week.
Why it's promising: Lawyers hate tracking their time, but law firms depend on those records to get paid. Laurel automates the chore while helping firms bill for work that might otherwise slip through the cracks.
More than 150 professional services firms use the software, including EY, Cozen O'Connor, and Husch Blackwell. Investor Miloni Madan Presler is especially excited about what comes next: Laurel is pushing beyond timekeeping to help firms decide how to price work and staff it.
Learned Hand
Nominated by: Ashley Mayer, Coalition (not an investor), and Zach Posner, The LegalTech Fund (not an investor)
Total funding: $5 million, according to the company
What it does: Learned Hand serves as a digital law clerk, helping judges and court staff review motions, research the law, and prepare rulings more quickly.
Why it's promising: Courts are drowning in paperwork and short on the clerks and research attorneys who help judges work through it. "This problem is only getting worse," Zach Posner said.
Learned Hand is already being used by court systems in 10 states, including the Michigan Supreme Court, and is being tested in Los Angeles County, home to one of the nation's largest court systems. Ashley Mayer called founder Shlomo Klapper, a former Second Circuit clerk and Palantir alum, the right person to build it.
Manifest OS
Nominated by: Max Abram, Scale Venture Partners (not an investor), and Yuanling Yuan, SignalFire (not an investor)
Total funding: $72 million, according to the company
What it does: Manifest is a tech-enabled law firm that recruits lawyers to practice under its banner. The attorneys handle client matters and legal strategy, while Manifest runs the back office and builds software to streamline their work.
Why it's promising: Just as Uber put more drivers on the road by making it easier to earn money behind the wheel, Manifest is trying to help more people get legal services by enabling lawyers to take on more clients. The company started with business immigration and says it has processed more than 3,000 visa cases to date.
Investors are betting it can repeat that playbook in practice areas far beyond immigration. Manifest has "absolutely crushed its first market," Yuanling Yuan said, calling chief executive Dan Mishin "a beast of a founder."
Patlytics
Nominated by: Yuanling Yuan, SignalFire (an investor)
Total funding: $65 million, according to the company
What it does: Patlytics builds software for law firms and in-house legal teams to manage patent work. Its tools cover the full lifecycle, from invention disclosures and filings to portfolio management and litigation.
Why it's promising: As Harvey and Legora battle to become the central workspace for lawyers, another group of startups is going deeper, building software for highly specialized areas of legal work. Patlytics is betting that patent law is one of those areas where a broad tool may not be enough.
That bet appears to be paying off. "The growth has been exceptional," investor Yuanling Yuan said. Patlytics works with nearly half of the 100 highest-grossing law firms in the country, such as Latham & Watkins, Morrison Foerster, and Quinn Emanuel.
Sandstone
Nominated by: Abby Meyers, Bain Capital Ventures (not an investor), and Jimena Nowack, IVP (not an investor)
Total funding: $40 million, according to the company
What it does: Sandstone helps in-house legal departments tame the flood of requests coming their way. Its software connects with a company's existing systems and draws on its contracts and other legal data to draft and review agreements, answer questions, and route matters to the right person.
Why it's promising: Corporate legal teams are under pressure to do more work without adding headcount, making them eager for tools that help them move faster. That makes them a strong market for Sandstone.
Demand is already taking off. Sandstone said revenue grew more than fortyfold over a recent 90-day period, with customers such as Wayfair, Mercury, MasterClass, and ElevenLabs. To keep pace, it raised a $30 million Series A led by Lightspeed in June, just six months after closing a $10 million seed led by Sequoia.
Solve Intelligence
Nominated by: Mallun Yen, Operator Collective (an investor)
Total funding: $55 million, according to the company
What it does: Solve Intelligence helps patent teams draft filings, respond to inquiries, and analyze their portfolios. It aims to become the command center where law firms and clients collaborate across every stage of patent work.
Why it's promising: Patent work has become one of legal tech's newest battlegrounds, thanks to its enormous market and mountains of paperwork. Solve Intelligence is pulling ahead and profitable, said Mallun Yen, who backed the company in a $40 million round in December.
Yen spent two decades as a law firm attorney and general counsel, working with patents from both sides of the table. "I know exactly how little patience this industry has for tools that don't hold up under scrutiny," she said. Solve is the first tool she has seen patent attorneys trust from start to finish.
Soxton
Nominated by: Ashley Mayer, Coalition (an investor)
Total funding: $2.5 million, according to the company
What it does: Soxton is an AI-enabled law firm that helps startups handle the routine legal work of getting a company off the ground.
Why it's promising: Most founders cannot justify hiring an expensive law firm on day one, but relying on ChatGPT for legal advice carries obvious risks. Soxton is trying to fill that gap with fast, affordable help from real lawyers. Founder Logan Brown knows the market firsthand from her time as a Cooley attorney.
Early demand suggests the need is real. More than 800 companies have used Soxton since it launched in December, and its waitlist stretches into the thousands, said Mayer, who backed the company at seed.
Wexler
Nominated by: Zach Posner, The LegalTech Fund (an investor)
Total funding: $7 million, according to the company
What it does: Wexler's software helps litigators find answers in case documents and verify that the evidence supports their arguments. The goal is to let them move faster and build stronger cases.
Why it's promising: Global firms like HSF Kramer, Goodwin Procter, and Clifford Chance have adopted the software, and Wexler says it has cut manual review time by 75% in some cases. The company is now pushing further into the courtroom: Its newest tool can flag false or conflicting testimony in real time during depositions and hearings.
articles.summary:
Artificial intelligence is transforming the legal industry as venture capitalists pour billions into startups designed to automate complex attorney tasks, streamline litigation, and improve access to legal services.

### Key Points
- Global investment in legal technology startups reached $2.1 billion during the first half of the year as AI models accelerated software adoption.
- Companies like Atticus and Manifest OS are scaling by automating legal aid for individuals and streamlining back-office operations for law firms.
- Specialized platforms including Patlytics and Solve Intelligence are targeting high-stakes patent law, while tools like Wexler and Courtroom assist with litigation and evidence review.
- In-house legal teams are increasingly adopting efficiency-focused software from providers like GC AI and Sandstone to manage rising workloads without increasing headcount.
- Startups such as Crosby and Soxton are blending AI software with human legal expertise to provide affordable, scalable services for early-stage companies.

### Why it Matters
The rapid integration of AI into legal workflows is collapsing traditional sales cycles and forcing a shift in how law firms and corporate departments bill for their time. By automating routine tasks and document analysis, these startups are creating new competitive advantages that prioritize efficiency and data-driven decision-making over traditional hourly billing models.

articles.title: How a Google Machine Terminated 130,000 AI Slop YouTube Channels in Six Months
articles.author: Alex Cooke
articles.content:
Google researchers have published a paper laying out the system YouTube appears to be using to delete AI spam in bulk, and the numbers are large. Over six months, the system terminated 50,000 clusters covering 130,000 channels.
The paper was surfaced and analyzed in Jim Louderback's newsletter Inside the Creator Economy, which flagged the shift in how enforcement now works. The system is called the Scalable Cluster Termination System, or S-CTS, and it comes from a paper titled "Scalable Detection of Adversarial Synthetic Slop and Coordinated Media Abuse: A LoRA-Enabled Multimodal Defense System." According to Search Engine Journal, the paper describes a production-oriented, two-stage machine learning system built to detect and terminate coordinated networks of accounts flooding video platforms with AI-generated spam. Google does not always confirm which research systems are actually deployed or where they run, so treat this as published research rather than a confirmed description of live YouTube infrastructure.
Here is the core change. Instead of grading one video at a time, S-CTS asks whether a group of accounts is sharing the same AI-generated template, and when enough accounts in an infrastructure cluster reuse the same semantic pattern, the entire cluster is terminated together. The researchers describe a content classifier that uses text embeddings to spot templated, scripted narratives and non-human publishing frequency, paired with an infrastructure component that groups accounts likely to share the same origin script or API. The paper reports a less than 1% overturn rate and a 32% reduction in cluster validation time compared to human review. The system also uses techniques like Low-Rank Adaptation to update defenses quickly when spammers switch to a new generative model, without retraining everything from scratch.
This lines up with what YouTube has said publicly. In his January 2026 letter, CEO Neal Mohan wrote that to reduce the spread of low quality AI content, the company is "actively building on our established systems that have been very successful in combatting spam and clickbait, and reducing the spread of low quality, repetitive content," as Mohan put it. That is the same window in which reporting tracked 16 high-reach channels either wiped or removed, channels that collectively held roughly 35 million subscribers and 4.7 billion lifetime views. YouTube has taken pains to clarify that AI itself is not being prohibited. AI-assisted work with real human input and proper disclosure stays eligible for monetization; the target is mass-produced, templated content with no human creative contribution.
Louderback's read is that the same behaviors that make a legitimate media company efficient, like shared templates, synced upload schedules, and common infrastructure, can also make it look like a coordinated slop factory to a pattern-matching system. A 1% overturn rate sounds tiny, but 1% of 50,000 is still around 500 clusters, and that figure only counts creators with the resources to appeal and win. Anyone who never appealed, or appealed and lost, is not in that number. Even a successful appeal does not restore the subscribers, views, and algorithmic momentum lost while a channel sat dark. There is a second wrinkle worth watching: separate reporting has noted that YouTube's algorithm changes have tended to favor videos with real human faces on camera, which is not the same distinction as human-made versus AI-made. That would penalize faceless creators who produce everything themselves, from voiceover explainers to ambient content, without ever using AI.
The broader pattern extends past video. SEO analysts tracking hundreds of sites running scaled AI content have documented a recurring shape: rapid growth, an organic traffic peak, then a steep collapse once Google's systems gather enough signal. The paper even cites Sentence-BERT as a way to catch AI-generated text that has been reworded on the surface but keeps the same underlying structure, which suggests cluster-level logic could eventually reach beyond video.
